Title: Metabolism, signaling, and transport of jasmonates

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating OPDA export can tune wound-induced jasmonate production and resistance while potentially avoiding full fertility defects seen in core JA-biosynthetic knockouts. Useful for stress-resilience engineering, but partial JA reduction may compromise cold and pathogen resistance.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JASSY promotes chloroplast export of OPDA, enabling wound-induced JA accumulation and downstream defense responses.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; wound-induced JA metabolite profiling; subcellular localization; OPDA binding/channel evidence
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Outer chloroplast envelope Bet v1-like channel; jassy mutants retain fertility and accumulate small amounts of JA, implying basal OPDAH diffusion can support floral JA needs.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces JA accumulation and increases susceptibility to cold and pathogens.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JASSY binds OPDA and functions as a chloroplast envelope channel. 2. It promotes OPDA export required for rapid wound-induced JA biosynthesis. 3. Its loss compromises defense-related JA accumulation but does not abolish fertility, indicating residual basal diffusion.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Cytosol and nucleus for signaling-active jasmonates; leaves under wound response
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, wound response, pathogen resistance, stress adaptation, reproductive development

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate leverage point for engineering plastidial OPDA export and JA biosynthesis in woody species; should be combined with pathway and transport analyses before deployment because substrate scope remains uncertain.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OPDAT1 may participate in plastid inner-envelope export of OPDA for JA biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Localization and functional identification referenced in review
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: JA biosynthesis context; likely stress-responsive, not detailed here
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Whether OPDAT1 also transports dn-OPDA remains unresolved.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OPDAT1 was identified as a plastid envelope protein linked to OPDA export. 2. It is proposed to support JA biosynthesis by moving OPDA out of plastids. 3. Its role in dn-OPDA export remains unclear.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, wound response, stress adaptation

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Engineering CTS offers a way to rebalance OPDA versus JA pools and alter defense output, but may provoke delayed JA responses and accumulation of alternative OPDA-dependent signals; use carefully in crop defense design.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CTS facilitates peroxisomal OPDA import, promoting conversion of OPDA to JA and balancing OPDA/JA homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; wound-induced time-course metabolite profiling; physiological phenotyping
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ABCD full-size transporter; transport activity and exact substrate remain to be confirmed in planta. cts mutants accumulate reduced but not abolished JA, supporting basal OPDA diffusion into peroxisomes.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced and delayed JA accumulation; elevated OPDA and ketols may shift signaling balance.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CTS is implicated in peroxisomal import of OPDA for JA biosynthesis. 2. cts mutants show reduced basal and wound-induced JA but elevated OPDA, indicating altered flux partitioning. 3. CTS therefore acts as a node controlling OPDA-to-JA conversion and JA-independent OPDA signaling balance.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Peroxisome-derived JA in leaves, cytosol, and nucleus after transport
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, wound response, fertility, stress adaptation

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: 13-LOX activity is a leverage point to increase JA production in stress-responsive tissues, but flux competition with 9-LOX oxylipins and possible growth-defense tradeoffs should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxygenates α-linolenic acid to form 13-hydroperoxy octadecatrienoic acid (13-HPOT).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtLOX2/3/4/6 supply the initial committed oxylipin precursor pool for chloroplast JA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway assignment; localization studies; metabolite pathway analysis summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding and stress contexts influence JA pathway flux
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified here; chloroplast-localized isoforms in Arabidopsis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Distribution of α-LeA between chloroplast and cytosol modulates flux between 13-LOX-derived JAs and 9-LOX-derived oxylipins.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Redirecting flux may reduce other oxylipins or alter defense balance.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Upstream flux-control step; not explicitly established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Chloroplast 13-LOXs initiate JA biosynthesis from α-linolenic acid. 2. They determine entry of fatty-acid flux into the JA-producing branch. 3. Compartmental substrate distribution influences competition with cytosolic 9-LOX oxylipin synthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, wound responses, developmental regulation

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AOS is a key target to elevate or suppress JA production; useful in defense engineering but strong perturbation may affect fertility and growth-defense balance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts 13-HPOT into unstable allene oxide intermediates in the octadecanoid pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AOS channels 13-LOX products into JA biosynthesis rather than competing CYP74-derived oxylipins.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway elucidation summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AOS is a CYP74 family enzyme competing with HPL, epoxyalcohol synthase, and DES for 13-HPOT.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux redirection away from other oxylipins may alter defense spectrum.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Allene oxide intermediate leading to OPDA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOS converts 13-HPOT into the JA-specific allene oxide intermediate. 2. It commits chloroplast oxylipin flux toward OPDA production. 3. Its proximity to AOC supports efficient stereospecific jasmonate precursor formation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AOC is suited for fine-tuning stereospecific OPDA formation and JA output, potentially in combination with AOS/OPR3 stacking for stronger defense traits.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes the unstable allene oxide intermediate to form cis-(+)-OPDA with defined stereochemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AOC establishes the stereochemical form of OPDA that feeds productive JA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AOC proximity to AOS product is required for rapid cis-(+)-OPDA formation and stereochemical fidelity retained in downstream jasmonates.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important stereochemical control step; not explicitly rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AOC converts the unstable AOS product into cis-(+)-OPDA. 2. It establishes stereochemistry preserved in later JA pathway intermediates. 3. Efficient JA precursor production depends on close coupling with AOS.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OPR3 is a major engineering target for modulating JA output, but strong suppression can trigger alternative OPR2-dependent routes rather than complete JA elimination.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces the cyclopentenone ring of OPDA to form OPC-8:0.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OPR3 drives the canonical peroxisomal conversion of OPDA toward JA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; subcellular targeting; biochemical pathway evidence
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Environmental conditions may determine preference for OPR3-dependent versus alternative OPR2-dependent route
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Among six Arabidopsis OPRs, only OPR3 is peroxisomal. An alternative OPR3-independent route can still yield JA via OPR2 after formation of 4,5-ddh-JA.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss may not abolish JA completely because alternative pathway compensates under some conditions.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point in canonical pathway
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OPR3 is the canonical peroxisomal reductase converting OPDA to OPC-8:0. 2. It is the only peroxisome-targeted OPR in Arabidopsis. 3. OPR3 loss uncovers an alternative OPR2-dependent JA pathway, showing pathway plasticity.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OPR2 should be considered when engineering JA deficiency because alternative-pathway compensation can preserve JA production; stacking with OPR3 edits may be needed for stronger suppression.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces 4,5-didehydro-JA to JA in the alternative OPR3-independent pathway after cytosolic release.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OPR2 supports an alternative ancient JA biosynthesis route when OPR3 activity is absent.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant pathway dissection and metabolite analysis summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Distinct environmental conditions may favor this retained ancient pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Proposed ancient route retained in vascular plants; OPR3-dependent route is generally preferred.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Compensatory JA formation may complicate breeding for low-JA traits.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OPR2 can reduce 4,5-ddh-JA to JA in an alternative route. 2. This pathway becomes evident when OPR3 is absent. 3. It suggests evolutionary conservation of a noncanonical JA biosynthetic mechanism.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for tuning canonical JA flux downstream of OPR3. Likely best manipulated with other β-oxidation steps because redundancy may buffer single-gene perturbation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Activates OPC-8:0 by esterifying it to CoA before β-oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OPCL1 enables entry of OPC-8:0 into peroxisomal β-oxidation toward JA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical and genetic evidence summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Required pathway step; rate-limiting not established
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OPCL1 converts OPC-8:0 into its CoA ester. 2. This activation is required before β-oxidation can yield JA. 3. It links OPDA reduction to peroxisomal chain-shortening steps.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ACX enzymes contribute to β-oxidation-driven JA production; due to family redundancy, multi-gene strategies may be needed for effective engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes the first oxidation step(s) of peroxisomal β-oxidation converting OPC-8:0-CoA intermediates toward JA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ACX supports peroxisomal β-oxidation rounds required to remove six carbons and generate JA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic and biochemical pathway evidence summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product through precursor shortening
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts sequentially with MFP and KAT in three rounds of β-oxidation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Potential broader effects on fatty-acid β-oxidation if manipulated strongly.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Shortened β-oxidation intermediates en route to JA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACX catalyzes β-oxidation reactions in the terminal phase of JA biosynthesis. 2. Together with MFP and KAT, it shortens OPC-derived CoA esters to JA. 3. It is essential for canonical peroxisomal JA production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: An additional β-oxidation target to tune JA biosynthesis; likely requires combinatorial manipulation with ACX/KAT due to pathway integration.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes hydration/dehydrogenation reactions of β-oxidation intermediates during JA formation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MFP participates in peroxisomal β-oxidation steps converting OPC-derived intermediates to JA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic and biochemical pathway evidence summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product through precursor shortening
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May affect broader lipid catabolism if perturbed.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Further shortened β-oxidation intermediates en route to JA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MFP catalyzes central β-oxidation reactions in JA biosynthesis. 2. It acts with ACX and KAT to shorten OPC-derived intermediates. 3. It is part of the canonical peroxisomal route to JA.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful downstream target for reducing JA accumulation, but broad peroxisomal lipid effects and developmental penalties should be considered.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes thiolytic cleavage in β-oxidation rounds producing chain-shortened intermediates leading to JA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: KAT completes peroxisomal β-oxidation cycles required for JA formation from OPC derivatives.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic and biochemical pathway evidence summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product through precursor shortening
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts in sequential β-oxidation rounds removing six carbons overall from OPC-derived chain.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely broader effects on peroxisomal fatty-acid breakdown.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Chain-shortened acyl-CoA intermediates culminating in JA
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. KAT catalyzes the thiolase step of β-oxidation in JA biosynthesis. 2. It helps complete three rounds of chain shortening from OPC-derived intermediates. 3. Its action is necessary for canonical JA production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JAR1 is a premier target for controlling bioactive JA-Ile levels and defense signaling. Partial suppression can preserve fertility because residual JA-Ile is produced by other GH3s; complete pathway shutoff may require multiplex editing.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Conjugates JA with isoleucine to form JA-Ile in the cytosol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JAR1 produces the major bioactive jasmonate JA-Ile, enabling robust COI1-dependent signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; biochemical enzyme assignment; crystallization/structural evidence; JA-Ile quantification
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Member of GH3 family. jar1 mutants retain 10%–20% JA-Ile and remain fertile, suggesting other GH3 enzymes contribute to residual conjugation.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing JAR1 weakens defense signaling; residual pathways complicate phenotype prediction.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step for bioactive JA-Ile production
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JAR1 catalyzes JA conjugation to isoleucine, generating the principal bioactive jasmonate. 2. It strongly affects JA-responsive gene expression and floral development. 3. Residual JA-Ile in jar1 mutants indicates partial redundancy by other GH3 enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Core jasmonate signal for defense, wound response, fertility

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suppressing JOX/JAO activity could prolong JA accumulation and strengthen defense, whereas overexpression could reduce fitness costs of excessive JA signaling. Tradeoffs with growth are likely.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates JA to produce 12-hydroxy-JA, contributing to JA inactivation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JOX/JAO enzymes directly catabolize JA and attenuate jasmonate signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene family characterization and metabolite pathway assignment summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Members of the 2-oxoglutarate Fe(II)-dependent oxygenase family.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated JA due to reduced catabolism may impair growth or alter development.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JOX/JAO enzymes hydroxylate JA directly to 12-OH-JA. 2. This reaction contributes to JA inactivation and homeostasis. 3. They represent an inducible catabolic arm of jasmonate metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling; its catabolism attenuates response duration

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CYP94 genes are promising targets to extend or attenuate JA-Ile signaling duration. Manipulation may improve defense durability but risks overactivation and growth penalties.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: CYP94B1/B3 hydroxylate JA-Ile to 12-OH-JA-Ile, and CYP94C1 further oxidizes it to 12-COOH-JA-Ile.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CYP94 enzymes deactivate JA-Ile and help terminate jasmonate signaling.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical pathway assignment; metabolite profiling summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wound response; rapid rise and decline of JA-Ile in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Localized to ER where JA-Ile catabolism occurs; 12-OH-JA-Ile is also bioactive in some contexts.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced catabolism may prolong defense at cost of growth; enhanced catabolism may weaken immunity.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CYP94B1/B3/C1 oxidize JA-Ile to less active catabolites. 2. Their ER-localized activity contributes to rapid turnover of JA-Ile after induction. 3. They are central to terminating and shaping JA signaling duration.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Core jasmonate signal; catabolism tunes defense intensity and duration

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: IAR3/ILL6 can be edited to prolong JA-Ile signaling or co-optimized with auxin pathways because these hydrolases mediate hormone crosstalk. Careful tuning is needed to avoid broad hormonal imbalance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydrolyze JA-Ile or 12-OH-JA-Ile to release JA or 12-OH-JA plus isoleucine.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: IAR3 and ILL6 turn over JA-Ile, attenuating jasmonate signaling and contributing to 12-OH-JA formation after wounding.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; wound-induced metabolite profiling; biochemical amidohydrolase assays summarized in review
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Wound inducible (from cited studies referenced in review)
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Operate in ER-associated JA-Ile catabolism and can simultaneously affect auxin homeostasis according to cited literature.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Hormone crosstalk with auxin may produce pleiotropic effects.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IAR3 and ILL6 hydrolyze JA-Ile and related conjugates. 2. They contribute to hormone turnover and wound-induced formation of 12-OH-JA. 3. Their activity helps reset jasmonate signaling and links JA with other hormone pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling; turnover controls response duration

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential route to modulate OPDA storage and availability for rapid JA bursts, but utility remains exploratory because vacuolar trafficking and physiological significance need further confirmation.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Conjugates OPDA with glutathione to form OPDA-GSH, proposed as a vacuolar storage form.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: GST6 may divert OPDA into a storage conjugate pool rather than immediate JA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Subcellular localization and conjugate identification referenced in review
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for storage/modification of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Potentially relevant in distal undamaged leaves during wound response
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OPDA-GSH is proposed to be transported into the vacuole; the transport system remains unverified.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May reduce immediately available OPDA for JA production.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. GST6 can conjugate OPDA with glutathione. 2. The resulting OPDA-GSH is proposed to represent a vacuolar storage form. 3. This branch may influence OPDA availability for rapid JA production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Precursor reservoir influencing jasmonate defense signaling

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: COI1 is a central switch for JA-Ile responsiveness and therefore a powerful but high-risk engineering target. Partial or tissue-specific modulation is preferable because strong perturbation can cause severe defense and fertility defects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: COI1 perceives JA-Ile within the SCFCOI1-JAZ coreceptor, triggering JAZ degradation and activation of JA-responsive transcription.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; coreceptor biochemistry; JA-Ile-dependent binding assays; signaling phenotypes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Master receptor component essential for converting JA-Ile accumulation into transcriptional response.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding, pathogen attack, developmental cues affecting JA-Ile levels
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Not directly reported for COI1 in this review
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Nuclear-localized; expression pattern not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Nuclear-localized; expression pattern not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Participates in negative feedback via induction of JAZ and JA-Ile catabolic genes after activation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Part of SCF E3 ubiquitin ligase coreceptor assembled in nucleus; can perceive multiple bioactive JA-amino acid conjugates.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong loss impairs defense and fertility.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: SCFCOI1 ubiquitin ligase complex with JAZ as coreceptor
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: Hormone receptor / F-box E3 ligase component
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly activates JA-responsive genes by promoting degradation of JAZ repressors
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. COI1 is the receptor component that senses JA-Ile together with JAZ. 2. JA-Ile-dependent COI1 activity causes JAZ degradation via the SCF complex. 3. This derepresses MYC transcription factors and activates core JA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Activates defense, wound, and reproductive jasmonate responses

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: JAZ proteins are attractive targets for reprogramming defense intensity and duration; stable or degradation-resistant alleles can dampen JA costs, while weakening JAZ repression can enhance defense. Must account for strong growth-defense tradeoffs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: JAZ proteins repress MYC-driven jasmonate responses until JA-Ile-induced COI1 signaling triggers their degradation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction studies; JA-Ile-dependent COI1-JAZ binding; degradation assays; mutant and transgenic signaling analyses
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Primary repressors that determine whether accumulated JA-Ile can activate transcriptional responses.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding, pathogen attack, developmental cues raising JA-Ile
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Indirect chromatin effects via recruitment of TPL/HDA-containing repression machinery
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Accumulate at low JA-Ile levels; stable JAZ proteins can be induced as feedback
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Negative feedback because JA signaling induces JAZ genes, restoring repression
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: COI1; MYC2; MYC3; MYC4; NINJA; TPL
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Members of TIFY family; stable JAZ variants contribute to response termination.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced repression may enhance defense but inhibit growth and reproduction; excessive repression weakens immunity.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: SCFCOI1-JAZ receptor complex and JAZ-NINJA-TPL repressor complex
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional repression relieved by ubiquitin-mediated degradation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses JA-responsive genes indirectly by inhibiting MYCs
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. JAZ proteins repress MYC activators at low JA-Ile levels. 2. JA-Ile perception by SCFCOI1 triggers their degradation. 3. Reinduction of JAZ genes forms a negative-feedback loop that resets signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Switches transcriptional defense and developmental programs

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MYC2/3/4 are strategic nodes for boosting JA-responsive defense and secondary metabolism, but activation often causes growth penalties. Tissue-specific or inducible tuning is recommended.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MYC2, MYC3, and MYC4 activate a large portion of JA-responsive genes after release from JAZ repression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic analyses; DNA-binding to G-box motifs; interaction studies with JAZ and MED25 summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major transcriptional switches translating JA-Ile perception into defense gene activation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: JA-Ile accumulation after wounding, pathogen attack, developmental cues
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Transcriptional activation is strengthened by recruitment of coactivators including HAC1 and LUH
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detailed here; function in JA-responsive tissues
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Embedded in feedback through induction of JAZ and catabolic genes after activation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional activation of jasmonate response programs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Subclade IIIe bHLH proteins binding G-box motifs.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Large set of JA-responsive genes including JAZ and JA-Ile catabolic genes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Associates with Mediator and coactivators after JAZ removal
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MYC2/3/4 are core activators of JA-responsive transcription. 2. They are repressed by JAZ at low JA-Ile and released upon JAZ degradation. 3. They engage MED25 and additional coactivators to activate downstream genes.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, stress responses, developmental regulation
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: JAZ degradation following COI1 perception of JA-Ile

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: NINJA offers a moderate lever for weakening JA repression without abolishing perception. Suitable for fine-tuning defense if pleiotropic transcriptional effects are managed.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NINJA links JAZ repressors to TPL corepressor machinery, reinforcing repression of JA-responsive genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction evidence summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Adaptor required for efficient assembly of the JA repressor complex at low JA-Ile.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Low JA-Ile conditions versus induced states
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Indirectly linked to chromatin repression through TPL and histone deacetylase recruitment
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functions as adaptor rather than DNA-binding TF.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Weakening repression may elevate constitutive defense and growth costs.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JAZ-associated repression modules acting on MYC targets
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly represses JA-responsive genes by recruiting TPL via JAZ
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NINJA bridges JAZ proteins to TPL corepression machinery. 2. It helps maintain the repressed state of JA-responsive genes at low JA-Ile. 3. Its function is released when JA-Ile promotes JAZ degradation.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Controls activation threshold of JA-responsive transcription
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Low JA-Ile state via JAZ stability

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: TPL-centered repression can be manipulated for broader defense rewiring, but because TPL participates in many pathways it is not a selective JA engineering target.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: TPL acts as a scaffolding corepressor recruited by JAZ-NINJA to silence JA-responsive genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein complex model and interaction evidence summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key corepressor establishing the off-state of JA-responsive transcription before hormone accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Associated with histones, histone deacetylase, and Mediator interactions to maintain repressed chromatin/transcriptional state
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely severe pleiotropy if manipulated broadly.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA-responsive transcriptional machinery downstream of MYCs
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TPL is recruited by JAZ-bound NINJA to repress JA-responsive genes. 2. It associates with chromatin-related repressors and Mediator components. 3. Loss of JAZ repression upon JA-Ile perception relieves TPL-mediated silencing.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Determines transcriptional off-state of JA signaling
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Recruitment by JAZ-NINJA under low JA-Ile

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: MED25 is a useful downstream integrator for boosting JA-induced transcription, but broad effects on multiple pathways make it better suited for mechanistic engineering than routine breeding.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: MED25 binds MYC after JAZ degradation and helps assemble the transcription preinitiation complex for JA-responsive genes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Interaction studies and transcriptional mechanism summarized in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: A central coactivator at the nexus converting derepressed MYC into active transcription.
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Works with HAC1 and LUH to activate transcription, likely via chromatin remodeling/coactivation
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Contributes to induction of negative-feedback genes such as JAZ and catabolic enzymes
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MYC2; RNA polymerase II; HAC1; LUH
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional activation of jasmonate-responsive gene programs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mediator subunit at nexus of JA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad transcriptional roles may produce pleiotropy.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MED25 engages MYC after JAZ removal. 2. It helps recruit transcription machinery for JA-responsive genes. 3. It is a key coactivator node linking perception to gene activation.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: JA-Ile-triggered JAZ degradation and MYC exposure

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Chromatin-level coactivators such as HAC1 can enhance JA outputs but are likely pleiotropic; targeted or inducible deployment is preferable.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: HAC1 acts as a coactivator recruited during activation of JA-responsive genes after JAZ degradation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supports full transcriptional activation downstream of MYC and MED25.
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Histone acetyltransferase activity promotes active chromatin state
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as additional coactivator in JA transcription complex.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Likely pleiotropic due to broad chromatin roles.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HAC1 is recruited as a coactivator in activated JA transcription complexes. 2. Its histone acetyltransferase function likely facilitates chromatin accessibility. 3. It supports robust expression of JA-responsive genes.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: LUH may be useful for fine-tuning transcriptional output but is not a selective jasmonate-only engineering handle.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: LUH acts as a coactivator associated with activated MYC/MED25 transcription complexes in JA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Supports activation phase of JA-responsive transcription after derepression.
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Likely involved in transcriptional coactivation/chromatin modulation, but direction not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as additional coactivator in nucleus.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LUH is recruited during the activated state of JA signaling. 2. It contributes to full transcriptional activation downstream of JA-Ile perception. 3. It acts with Mediator-associated machinery rather than as a primary receptor or TF.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AtJAT1 is a high-value engineering target because it couples JA export with nuclear JA-Ile import, enabling tuning of defense intensity, systemic signaling, and fertility thresholds. Tissue-specific manipulation is advised to avoid compromising immunity or floral development.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtJAT1 mediates nuclear import of JA-Ile and cellular export of JA, thereby integrating jasmonate biosynthesis, signaling, and transport.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast transporter screen; loss-of-function mutants; isolated nuclei transport assays; cultured cell export assays; JA-responsive gene expression; JAZ1 degradation assays; pathogen resistance phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: A decisive transporter-controller that concentrates JA-Ile in the nucleus while removing JA from cytosol, strongly shaping signaling amplitude and metabolic equilibrium.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; floral development; Botrytis cinerea infection
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Probably under positive feedback regulation by JA-Ile signaling according to review conclusion
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Potential ABCG dimer partner(s); JAZ1 degradation pathway components indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Nuclear import of bioactive hormone, cellular export, signaling homeostasis, and cell-cell transport contribution
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also termed ABCG16 in original literature context though not stated here; likely localized at inner nuclear membrane and plasma membrane. Higher affinity for nuclear JA-Ile import than JA export.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss reduces JA-responsive gene expression, JAZ1 degradation, and Botrytis resistance; combined loss with jar1 causes male sterility.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Nuclear JA-Ile levels; indirectly SCFCOI1-JAZ signaling state
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely forms homo-/heterodimers with another half-size ABC transporter
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly activates JA-responsive genes by increasing nuclear JA-Ile; attenuates signaling by exporting JA under high cytosolic levels
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtJAT1 actively imports JA-Ile into the nucleus and exports JA from the cell. 2. This dual activity links metabolic equilibrium between JA and JA-Ile with signaling strength. 3. AtJAT1 is required for full JA-responsive gene activation, JAZ1 degradation, pathogen resistance, and maintenance of nuclear JA-Ile during floral development.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Nucleus for JA-Ile; apoplasm for exported JA
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense signaling, systemic communication, reproductive development
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Cytosolic JA and JA-Ile levels; likely JA-Ile signaling feedback

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AtJAT3/4 are promising targets for enhancing systemic wound resistance and phloem-mediated jasmonate signaling. Deployment should focus on vascular tissues to improve long-distance signaling without broadly altering local development.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtJAT3 and AtJAT4 mediate cellular import of JA at the plasma membrane and are required for systemic wound-induced JA/JA-Ile responses in distal leaves.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function double mutant analysis; wound systemic response assays; D5-JA tracing; grafting; expression and localization studies; pathogen resistance phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controllers of long-distance JA transmission, especially for systemic wound immunity rather than local responses.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Wounding; systemic leaf-to-leaf signaling; Botrytis cinerea challenge in distal leaves
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Primarily expressed in phloem parenchyma cells including companion cells
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: JA cellular import and phloem-associated cell-cell/long-distance transport
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No obvious developmental defects in atjat3;4 under normal conditions, showing cellular JA import is dispensable for basal development but crucial for systemic wound resistance.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss nearly abolishes systemic wound resistance/immunity in distal leaves while leaving local wound response largely normal.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA distribution between apoplasm, vascular cells, and sieve-tube elements; indirectly distal JA-Ile accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Proposed homo-/heterodimers of AtJAT3 and AtJAT4
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly promotes systemic induction of primary JA-responsive genes in distal tissues
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtJAT3 and AtJAT4 import JA at the plasma membrane. 2. They are essential for efficient leaf-to-leaf JA translocation and systemic wound-induced JA/JA-Ile accumulation. 3. Their activity mainly affects distal systemic defense rather than local wound signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Systemic wound signaling, immunity, defense coordination

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AtJAT2 is a speculative but potentially useful target for controlling peroxisome-to-cytosol JA export and thereby JA-Ile synthesis. More validation is needed before application.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtJAT2 is proposed to participate in peroxisomal export of JA, potentially influencing cytosolic JA available for JA-Ile synthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Subcellular localization evidence; functional role proposed in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Potential controller of JA supply from peroxisome to cytosol, but not yet established experimentally.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Peroxisomal JA export and precursor supply to JA-Ile synthesis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Genetic and biochemical data remain to be collected.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown due to lack of functional validation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA distribution between peroxisome and cytosol
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirect, via changing JA availability for JA-Ile signaling
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtJAT2 localizes to the peroxisome. 2. It is proposed to export JA from the peroxisome to the cytosol. 3. This could connect JA biosynthesis with cytosolic JA-Ile production, but direct proof is still lacking.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potential control of defense hormone partitioning

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AtJAT5 is a candidate JA exporter for future engineering of intercellular jasmonate movement, but its transport role remains unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtJAT5 may be involved in JA export at the plasma membrane.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Putative controller of JA cellular efflux, not experimentally established in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: JA distribution between cytosol and apoplasm
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtJAT5 is a plasma-membrane JAT family member. 2. It is proposed to participate in JA export. 3. Its substrate specificity and physiological role remain to be validated.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: These vacuole-related ABCGs are exploratory candidates for engineering vacuolar jasmonate storage/sequestration, but no jasmonate function is proven here.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtABCG17/18/19 are candidate JAT family members that may localize to vacuoles and potentially transport jasmonates.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Phylogenetic inference and localization mention in review
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Not established; included as putative future regulators of vacuolar jasmonate compartmentation.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Potential vacuolar sequestration/storage transport of jasmonates
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AtABCG19 appears vacuolar and associated with kanamycin resistance; JA substrate identity remains untested.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AtABCG17/18/19 form a JAT-family clade of interest. 2. They may localize to vacuoles and potentially transport jasmonates. 3. Their roles in JA biology remain speculative and require direct testing.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: These broad-substrate NPF transporters are not yet reliable JA-engineering targets because in planta jasmonate relevance is unresolved and substrate promiscuity is high.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NPF4.1/AIT3 and NPF2.10/GTR1 can transport JA-Ile and/or JA in heterologous systems, but their in planta roles in jasmonate transport remain uncertain.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Yeast two-hybrid-based screening; transport assays in yeast and Xenopus oocytes
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent and not yet established in planta due to broad substrate specificity.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Male fertility phenotype tested with exogenous GA3 versus JA
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Localized at plasma membrane; tissue expression not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Potential JA/JA-Ile uptake across plasma membrane
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: These transporters also move ABA, GAs, nitrate, and glucosinolates; GA3 but not JA restored gtr1 male fertility.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation would likely perturb multiple hormone/nutrient transport pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NPF4.1/AIT3 and NPF2.10/GTR1 can transport JA-Ile and/or JA in heterologous systems. 2. Their strong substrate promiscuity complicates interpretation of jasmonate-related phenotypes. 3. In planta roles in JA transport and signaling remain unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Potential uptake affecting signaling and transport
